summary: Cantrill provides updates on DTrace's growing momentum, including Sun's DTrace Challenge contest with surprisingly valuable prizes, the launch of formal DTrace training courses, and positive press coverage in the New York Times. He expresses pride in Solaris 10 being recognized as a 'generational shift' in operating systems. The post captures a moment of optimism at Sun as DTrace and Solaris 10 gain traction in the industry.
key_points: Sun launched the DTrace Challenge with significant prizes including HDTVs and laptops
Formal DTrace training courses are now available
New York Times coverage positioned Solaris 10 as critical to Sun's future
Jonathan Adams, who did the DTrace code review, joined Sun's blogging community
money_quotes: When this idea was first floated internally, I assumed that the prizes would, well, kinda suck. Maybe a T-shirt or a mug or some Solaris DVDs or whatever.
I can say with confidence that there are bodies of in-kernel DTrace code that are understood by only two people – Jonathan and me.
Markoff nailed it: Solaris 10 is indeed a 'generational shift' – and it's one that we on Team DTrace are very much proud to be a part of…
Needless to say, I've never been so bummed to see the 'employees and their families are inelgible' rider – even at his young age, I'm sure Tobin could have developed a winning D script for his Da-da!
key_insight: Solaris 10 and DTrace represented a genuine technological leap that Sun hoped would restore the company's competitive position against Linux and Windows.
